content:
3 min read
Just now

--

A year ago, knowing how to use AI effectively was a genuine advantage. People who learned tools like ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, or Bob early could accomplish in hours what others took days to finish. They became more productive, wrote better code, debugged faster, and naturally stood out in their teams.
That advantage is disappearing.
Today, almost every engineer uses AI in one form or another. Whether it's Bob, Codex, Claude Code, Cursor, or whatever comes next, everyone has access to excellent models. Asking an AI to write a function, explain a stack trace, generate tests, or create Kubernetes manifests is no longer a unique skill. In fact, many of these tasks can now be performed reasonably well by someone with very little engineering experience. The models have become good enough to understand product context, codebases, APIs, and even architectural patterns.
That raises an uncomfortable question. If your daily work is simply asking AI to complete engineering tasks one prompt at a time, what differentiates you from everyone else using the exact same tools?
I'm not saying AI makes engineers less valuable. Quite the opposite. I think it raises the bar for what engineering actually means.
The biggest mistake I see today is treating AI as an assistant instead of treating it as infrastructure. Most people open Cursor or Codex, ask it to do something, review the output, and repeat that process throughout the day. It's a faster way of working, but fundamentally it's still the same workflow we've followed for years. We've simply replaced Google searches and Stack Overflow with an LLM.
The real opportunity is to stop thinking in prompts and start thinking in workflows.
Take software testing as an example. Most engineers use AI to generate Playwright tests or help debug failures. That's useful, but it's only solving one step of the process. A better approach is to build an agent that understands your repository, deploys your pull request to a test environment, invokes Playwright through MCP, validates the application, collects screenshots if something fails, summarizes the results, and posts everything back to GitHub or Slack. At that point, AI isn't helping you write tests. It's running an entire validation pipeline without requiring you to supervise every step.
The same mindset applies to operations. Instead of asking AI why a deployment failed, an agent can collect logs from Loki, query Prometheus, compare the deployment against previous releases, inspect Kubernetes events, identify the probable root cause, and even suggest a rollback if confidence is high. You haven't automated one command. You've automated an investigation.
Once you start looking at problems this way, opportunities appear everywhere. Release validation, cloud cost optimization, security reviews, documentation updates, dependency management, infrastructure provisioning, incident response, and even code reviews can become orchestrated workflows instead of isolated prompts. The engineer's value shifts from executing individual tasks to designing systems that execute those tasks automatically.
This is why I believe the conversation has already changed. Last year everyone was excited about agents. This year the interesting discussions are about orchestration. That transition happened incredibly quickly, and I don't think it's accidental. Individual agents are becoming commodities. What matters now is how they work together, what tools they can invoke, what context they receive, and how they make decisions across an entire workflow.
Technology has always rewarded engineers who move one level higher. We moved from assembly to high-level languages, from manually configuring servers to infrastructure as code, and from manual deployments to CI/CD. AI is simply another step in that evolution. The engineers who will have the biggest impact over the next few years won't necessarily be the ones who write the best prompts. They'll be the ones who build systems where AI performs entire workflows while humans focus on the problems that still require engineering judgment.
Using AI isn't a skill anymore.
Building with AI is.
